chunk_summary: Strategy 1 — SEO for SaaS: Focus on bottom-of-funnel, conversion-focused pages. Create 'versus' landing pages (e.g., Webflow vs WordPress), list posts (e.g., best productivity apps), and blog content that maps to user search intent using a levels-of-awareness framework (problem aware → solution aware → product aware). Zapier drives 4.3M monthly visits mostly from blog posts targeting these keywords. For programmatic SEO, create landing pages at scale using combinations (e.g., Zapier's 70,000 integration pages).
content: when I talk about SEO I mean it in two different ways editorial SEO and programmatic SEO so for SAS companies there are a few different types of pages you want to create to maximize your SEO potential and these are going to be mostly conversion Focus Pages I'm not going to talk about creating content to drive a ton of traffic to your website but I'm going to be talking about creating content that actually people at the bottom of the funnel are going to be searching ... for example let's say we're webflow and we know that people are searching up things like webflow versus WordPress ... the first SEO strategy on the editorial side is actually creating these versus style landing pages and if you look at the URL here with webflow they have this versus subfolder and in each one they actually have their competitors ... besides versus Pages what you want to also go after our list posts so for example say you're a SaaS company that's a productivity app if you were to write a blog post around the keyword best productivity apps ... in this case zapier is an article that's a SAS company going after this keyword best productivity apps next strategy with SEO is to actually do like blog content and here you want to pay really close attention to the user search Journey ... I essentially developed this framework of going through the levels of awareness of when somebody is searching you know are they problem aware are they solution aware are they product aware or are they most aware and through those different stages you go through and identify what people are searching for at that stage ... zapier is a SAS company that does this extremely well ... they get around 4.3 million monthly visitors just from SEO ... now that's the content side let's quickly look at what the programmatic side looks like now if we look at zapier's apps section of their website they're essentially doing this play where they've created 70,000 landing pages leveraging two different brand names and being their integration ... they've done everything from brand names to categories ... basically creating this framework of brand or app name plus brand or app name and then they create a landing page based off of those combinations
questions_on_this_chunk: What are the best SEO pages for a SaaS company?
How do versus landing pages work for SaaS?
What is the levels of awareness framework for content?
How did Zapier scale to 70,000 programmatic SEO pages?

chunk_summary: Strategy 2 — Product-led courses: Create free courses around the problem your SaaS solves, where completing the course requires using your product. This acts as a bridge between content consumption and signup, increasing conversion. Example: Webflow's 'Ultimate Web Design Course' gated behind signup, promoted via SEO-optimized blog posts with inline CTAs.
content: now the next strategy is going to stay on the theme of creating content and this one's going to be creating product-led courses around your SAS product so what I mean by this is chances are your SAS company especially if you're in the B2B space or you're like a b2c tool for creators your tool basically solves a problem that businesses or creators are looking for ... you might be a platform like webflow and then you teach people how to build a website or how to design a website and in these cases the idea is that you're creating how-to pieces of content or tutorials on how to do a broad subject but in order to actually complete your tutorial they have to use your SAS product so for example webflow has the webflow University where you can go through and learn everything about how to design a portfolio how to create a website ... and one of the strategies that works really well with this is when you're actually leveraging SEO to drive traffic to your website and then what you do is you create a course as a gated piece of content and it acts as a bridge before somebody goes from landing on your website to actually signing up for your SAS product ... if I were to search how to learn web design I can see there's this article from webflow that we released a long time ago ... what we noticed is when we used to try to send people straight to signing up for web flow it is a really complex SAS product the conversion rate was really low so instead of going from straight landing on a piece of content to signing up for the SAS product you go from landing on a piece of content to consuming a free course to then that course basically showcasing the SAS product and that's how you can really improve conversions ... on this article if you actually scroll through we created these inline blog ads and this basically is a call to action to getting the 100 video course on web design for free and you hit start course and it goes into the webflow university ultimate web design course and then once you want to enroll you're presented to sign up for the product
questions_on_this_chunk: What are product-led courses in SaaS marketing?
How can a free course improve SaaS signup conversion?
How did Webflow use Webflow University as a marketing strategy?
What is the bridge strategy between content and signup?

chunk_summary: Strategy 3 — Growth loops: the concept where product usage naturally drives more users. Retention is the silent killer of SaaS; if users stay and love the product, they tell others. Example: Beehiiv's 'Powered by Beehiiv' footer in every newsletter sent, exposing the brand to millions of recipients and driving organic signups. Referral programs are another form of growth loop.
content: now the next strategy is creating growth Loops within your product and this is something that was coined famously by Brian Balfour who was the former VP of growth at HubSpot ... growth Loops are basically this new concept of kind of like the new marketing funnel at least in Silicon Valley and it's really based around like user retention so it's when the idea is that somebody signs up for your product they get activated and they start using your product and the idea is that if they love the product enough to become a repeat customer then there's a better chance that they're going to tell people about it and when they tell people about it new people sign up and it kind of creates this growth Loop and the one thing that I learned in this course that I took 5 years ago was that retention is the silent killer of many startups especially SAS startups if you can't retain your users or people are churning there's a good chance that no matter what you do with all your marketing efforts your company eventually is going to die ... one really cool example of a company that does a growth Loop really well that's like a marketing way of doing it that's pretty easy is the company beehive ... if we actually go all the way to the bottom we can see that there's a section that says powered by beehive and if I actually click on this it goes straight to The Beehive website now think about this I send my newsletter to 16,000 people every week ... beehive sends multiple multiple millions of emails every month so imagine how much exposure their brand gets when people are sending emails through their platform and I actually discovered beehive because an investor that I was following started his newsletter on beehive and I saw that in the footer it said powered by beehive ... clicked on that little powered by beehive button fell in love with the tool and I've been using it 3 years later so these product growth Loops can work really well
questions_on_this_chunk: What is a growth loop in SaaS?
Why is retention so important for SaaS startups?
How does Beehiiv's 'Powered by' footer work as a growth loop?
What are other examples of product-led growth loops?

chunk_summary: Strategy 4 — Launch on Product Hunt and Hacker News: Product Hunt is a Reddit-style platform where many major SaaS companies (Notion, Webflow) got their first users. Launch at midnight PST when products reset. Hacker News (by Y Combinator) is ideal for developer-heavy products. Webflow got its first few thousand users and YC funding by launching a beta on Hacker News.
content: all right the next one is pretty simple but it's launching on product hun or Hacker News so product hun is a place where people launch their products and as you can see it's got this very much like Reddit up vote style to it and actually a lot of big companies that you see today initially launched on product hunt and got their first like Kickstart with this platform for example webflow has launched on product hunt notion has launched on product hunt a lot of big other SAS companies have launch on product hunt ... the main thing is that you want to launch at midnight which is when a lot of the products basically reset for the next day there's also another site called Hacker News and Hacker News is by y combinator it's like their Reddit like news site where people post things around developer tools or news in the development space so if you're running a SAS product that is very like developer technical heavy this could be a great place to also launch your product for example webflow when they initially launched the beta version of the platform when they had their web flow playground they launched on Hacker News and that's how they got their initial first few thousand users and got funding by YC combinator and started building a great product it started with them basically launching on Hacker News so I know this stuff is very simple but it can be very powerful from basically going from absolutely nothing to getting maybe your first like 10 to 20 users of your product maybe even more
questions_on_this_chunk: How do I launch my SaaS on Product Hunt?
What time should I launch on Product Hunt?
How did Webflow use Hacker News to get early users?
Is Hacker News better than Product Hunt for developer tools?

chunk_summary: Strategy 5 — Manual outreach: Find ideal customers on LinkedIn, use tools like Hunter.io to get emails, and send hyper-personalized cold emails offering value (free courses, case studies, helpful guides) rather than asking for a call directly. Also join niche communities via TheHiveIndex.com where your ICPs hang out, participate authentically, and build relationships that lead to early customers.
content: now the next one is also pretty simple it's basically doing manual Outreach so you can use a tool like clay.com or apollo.io or really you can just go to LinkedIn and figure out who your ideal customers are for your SAS product this is going to work mostly for people that are basically more sales-driven SAS companies so a lot of like Enterprise or any sort of SAS company where you see that's like book a demo with us where there's a big deal size and you have to do a lot of Outreach this is a strategy to basically get your first few clients for your SAS product find your ideal customer go to LinkedIn figure out who they are figure out their website go to another tool like hunter.io Place their website within the hunter.io find their email and send them a super personalized email on basically you giving them something of value and again if you actually have used like the first two strategies on here where you've created content on your website for SEO or you have case studies on your website or you have a lot of free courses you can email these to potential prospects and just say hey here's a great guide that I think would help you based off whatever problem your SAS product solves ... we also do this for a lot of companies just like yours would love to hop on a call with you and basically just see if you can help them just purely giving value without asking much in return now you can also join communities so there's a website called the Hive index.com and what you can do is you can go to where maybe your ideal customer is ... join them interact with people talk with them and this is how you can probably get your first few customers for your SAS product again these are very much like early stage strategies but they're the ones that get you off the ground running
questions_on_this_chunk: How do I do manual outreach for SaaS sales?
What tools can I use for cold email outreach?
What should I send in a cold outreach email?
How can joining niche communities help acquire SaaS customers?

chunk_summary: Strategy 6 — Community marketing via webinars: Clearscope runs SEO webinars that attract their exact ICP. Gate registration to capture leads, run the live event, then upload recordings to YouTube. This creates high-value content, builds an email list, and feeds a YouTube content strategy — all from one effort.
content: now the next one is doing community marketing so there's actually a company called clear scope that I really like it's an SEO tool and I think they're an amazing example of a company that does community marketing right so clear scope has their clear scope webinars and it's basically around everything related to search and if you work as an SEO manager or content marketing manager anybody that deals with SEO chances are if you go on their webinars page and you start reading through some of the headlines of these they are going to look super enticing and I don't think there's any other company that I've joined so many of their webinars other than clear scope and they've done a great job of just curating people in the SEO Community and getting them on their webinars ... so whatever your SAS product is if you can find a way to create a community around webinars and get people to join those webinars I guess it's almost like how people do podcasts these days but then if you can get webinars in it'll be a really cool way to do marketing and the thing I really like about this strategy is it's a way to actually build up leads for your business and then you can leverage email marketing in the future ... on their most latest webinar for Clear scope as you can see they have the date to register and you have to basically type in your name and your work email and then register and you would get a zoom link ... and once the webinars actually end up happening and they're live after that they actually end up using those videos and uploading them straight to YouTube so you always have them as well and this pretty much adds to your YouTube content strategy not only did you just create a great piece of content that can go on YouTube but you actually held off first and you turned it into a webinar that was gated and now you actually have leads and a list of emails from people that you can actually reach out to and potentially get them to become customers of your SAS product
questions_on_this_chunk: How can webinars be used for SaaS community marketing?
What is Clearscope's webinar strategy?
How do I turn a live webinar into YouTube content?
How can gated webinars build an email list for SaaS?

chunk_summary: Strategy 7 — Start a newsletter / become a media company: SaaS companies are acquiring media assets (HubSpot acquired The Hustle, Semrush acquired Backlinko) because owning audience attention is powerful. Start a newsletter curating news and insights in your industry, nurture trust, and organically promote your product. Backlinko content now funnels readers to Semrush.
content: which then this leads us nicely into email marketing and essentially starting a newsletter I think the future for SAS companies and I've been saying this for many years now is SAS companies are turning into media companies and if they're not turning into media companies they're acquiring media companies for example HubSpot a huge SAS company acquired the hustle which was a huge newsletter around business we also saw another company that's publicly traded semrush it's an SEO tool and they actually ended up acquiring backlinko.com which was a huge website around SEO still is that Brian Dean the founder of it created and if we actually look at backlinko this site gets like a million visitors a month there's essentially an email list that you can join and you bet that in any sort of piece of content where they're talking about a keyword research tool semrush has inserted themselves into this name leveraging the existing brand backlinko has built over the years so if you can start a newsletter around your industry and basically be the person that curates news in your industry or has really thoughtful original insights and get people to just learn things from you and you can nurture your community this is a great way to actually drive people and attention to your SAS business
questions_on_this_chunk: Why are SaaS companies turning into media companies?
How did HubSpot and Semrush use media acquisitions for growth?
How can a newsletter support SaaS marketing?
What is the Backlinko-Semrush acquisition strategy?

chunk_summary: Strategy 8 — Affiliate programs: Use tools like FirstPromoter, Rewardful, or PartnerStack. Offer a high commission for the first year (e.g., 50% for 12 months) to incentivize creators and publishers, while protecting long-term revenue. Webflow and Beehiiv both use 50% first-year commissions. Affiliates also passively build backlinks and domain authority.
content: now the next strategy is to create an affiliate program for your SAS company and there's a handful of tools that you can use specifically for SAS in the space I've seen SAS companies using first promoter I've also seen them using rewardful and for larger SAS companies like webflow or notion I've seen them use things like partner stack and these are great platforms to build out your affiliate program with and once you have an affiliate program you can get existing customers or different Publishers to promote your products and services and give them a commission now some of my favorite companies that do this really well are webflow and beehive they both have a commission structure of 50% for the first year of any paying customer and it's the 50% is really good because it creates this incentive for the Creator or the publisher that 50% is a lot for software company but then it's only for 12 months so the incentive on the SAS company's side is that they only have to pay out for a customer for up to one year so if that customer stays for 2 3 4 years say they stay for four years they're actually only paying the commissions on one of those years and the other three years is like a full customer to that actual SAS company ... this is actually a great way to even passively build backlinks and build your domain Authority if you're investing in things like editorial SEO and programmatic SEO
questions_on_this_chunk: What commission structure works best for SaaS affiliates?
How do Webflow and Beehiiv structure their affiliate programs?
What tools can I use to set up a SaaS affiliate program?
How can affiliates help with SEO and backlinks?

chunk_summary: Strategy 9 — A great SaaS homepage: structure it around three core questions from the book Breakthrough Advertising. 1) Desire: does it satisfy my desires? (Headline must speak to the prospect's deepest want, e.g., Webflow's 'Your site should do more than look good'). 2) Role: does it work for my job? (Feature/benefit section proving it fulfills their role). 3) Beliefs: is anyone else using it? (Social proof: testimonials, case studies, logos, e.g., Asana's 'trusted by world's top companies'). Ghost's 'product you can depend on' messaging is another example of aligning with audience beliefs.
content: last but not least I want to talk about creating a great homepage now you might be asking hey this isn't a marketing strategy but everything we've been talking about is about driving people to your website and to learn about your brand and what good does it do if people land on your website and have no idea what your product does or who it's for what problem it solves and so you need a great SAS homepage ... really if you want to think about just your homepage you need to think about three core things so when someone lands on your homepage they're going to kind of ask themselves three things one does this product satisfy my desires two does this product work does it fulfill my role and three is anyone else actually using this product do I believe this is the best product I can use to satisfy my desire and all this comes straight out of the book breakthrough advertising ... if we go back to the first one does this product satisfy my desires basically desire is defined by the wants and needs that drive your target audience through life ... for example webflow's homepage their hero section especially their H1 heading says your site should do more than look good now it's really cool that they're using the word your because it's speaking directly to the person that's landing on the website it's also speaking to the desire of their website visitors they know that people that are going to be using webflow they want to create a website that's fully custom but at the same time they want to have all the tools they need to make sure that they can grow their website ... next for your homepage you want to pay attention to the job roles that somebody has ... with your homepage this is mostly your feature benefit section for example HRS the tool that I showed you the different SEO data earlier in this video has a really great section on roles where they're showing the features and the benefits of their product ... a great way to figure this out is actually go and look at job postings for product marketing managers and see what the roles are and the requirements for that role and start speaking to what it says in those job descriptions ... okay now let's go on to the last part of your homepage beliefs ... you need to instill trust in your target audience so they believe that your product is for them essentially you need to give them social proof and prove that your SAS product is the best one available on the market to fit their role assana is an example of a SAS company that does this really well if you scroll all the way down their homepage you can see that they have this section on the world's top companies trust Asana with all their case studies ... another great example of this is Ghost so ghost is a CMS tool if you actually scroll all the way down on their homepage they have this section that says a product you can depend on ghost is open source independent and funded 100% by users no investors no BS and as you can see here they're really emphasizing on a product you can depend on and this does a great job of explaining that no other product on the market satisfies their audience's desire like theirs does
questions_on_this_chunk: What are the three core elements of a great SaaS homepage?
How should a SaaS homepage headline be written?
What is the desire-role-belief framework for landing pages?
How can I use social proof effectively on my homepage?
